course_subtitle: Ethernet for Scale-Up Networks — a deep-technical course on the ESUN Base Specification, Revision 1.0.

sources.path: OCP ESUN - Network Operator Requirements - Base Specification Rev 1.0 FINAL.pdf

modules.title: OCP ESUN: Ethernet for Scale-Up Networks
modules.subtitle: Why scale-up Ethernet exists, what changes at the wire, and what your hardware has to do to conform.

slides.title: What you'll learn
slides.subtitle: By the end of this 20-minute course, you'll be able to…
objectives: Position scale-up against scale-out and front-end networking.
Name the three trends that drove the industry to a dedicated scale-up spec.
Walk the 4-byte ESUN Header field by field, and explain what each field replaces.
Describe the fabric story end-to-end — PFC, CBFC, ECN, LLR, Flow Label, TTL.
List the endpoint and switch conformance requirements, plus where security fits.

slides.title: Scale-up networks in AI systems
slides.subtitle: Three networks coexist in a modern AI cluster. Don't confuse them.

bullets.text: users, storage, and the outside world. Looks like a normal data-center network.
joins racks for collective ops across hundreds-to-thousands of nodes.
the tightly coupled fabric inside what the developer treats as one giant accelerator.
model-parallel — small messages, frequent sync, no tolerance for jitter or loss.
from 8 GPUs/node (Llama 4) to ≥1,000 GPUs across racks (next-gen).

figure.alt: Scale-up vs scale-out vs front-end network topology
figure.caption: Front-end, scale-up, and scale-out networks shown across two racks of CPU+Accel nodes. Source: OCP ESUN Summit deck.

slides.title: Why ESUN, and why now
slides.subtitle: Three trends push scale-up beyond what standard Ethernet was built for.

bullets.text: developers want the whole scale-up domain to feel like one accelerator — the network can never make the compute wait.
GPUs are expensive; fused kernels need deterministic, low-overhead transport. Every byte of header saved is a byte of tensor moved.
as domains grow, blast radius grows with them. A 20% BW drop in a 128-accelerator domain → ~5% e2e perf loss.
evolve Ethernet rather than replace it. Stay on existing optics, silicon, and tools.

<svg viewBox="0 0 780 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width:100%;height:auto;" role="img" aria-label="Scale jump from 8 GPUs to 1,000 GPUs">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrowhead" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3, 0 6" fill="#6FA030"/>
    </marker>
    <style>
      .ttl { font: 600 14px 'Open Sans', system-ui, sans-serif; fill: #1a1a1a; }
      .sub { font: 400 11px 'Open Sans', system-ui, sans-serif; fill: #5F6062; }
      .big { font: 800 36px 'Open Sans', system-ui, sans-serif; fill: #6FA030; }
      .tag { font: 600 12px 'Open Sans', system-ui, sans-serif; fill: #6FA030; }
    </style>
  </defs>

  <!-- LEFT: Llama 4 era - single node, 8 GPUs -->
  <text x="140" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ttl">Llama 4 era</text>
  <text x="140" y="48" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">single node, single backplane</text>
  <rect x="30" y="60" width="220" height="180" rx="10" fill="#f5f7fa" stroke="#d0d5db" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- 8 GPU icons in 2 rows of 4 -->
  <g>
    <rect x="50" y="80" width="38" height="38" rx="4" fill="#8DC63F" stroke="#6FA030"/>
    <rect x="100" y="80" width="38" height="38" rx="4" fill="#8DC63F" stroke="#6FA030"/>
    <rect x="150" y="80" width="38" height="38" rx="4" fill="#8DC63F" stroke="#6FA030"/>

    <rect x="50" y="130" width="38" height="38" rx="4" fill="#8DC63F" stroke="#6FA030"/>

  </g>
  <text x="140" y="210" text-anchor="middle" class="big">8</text>
  <text x="140" y="232" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">GPUs in scale-up domain</text>

  <!-- ARROW -->
  <line x1="268" y1="150" x2="395" y2="150" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrowhead)"/>
  <text x="332" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="tag">ESUN target</text>
  <text x="332" y="172" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">multi-rack scale-up</text>

  <!-- RIGHT: next-gen, ≥1000 GPUs across many racks -->
  <text x="580" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ttl">Next-generation platforms</text>

  <text x="580" y="358" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">GPUs in scale-up domain</text>
</svg>
figure.alt: Scale jump from 8 GPUs/node to >=1000 GPUs across racks
figure.caption: Llama 4 era: 8 GPUs in a single chassis. Next-gen target: ≥1,000 GPUs across racks.

slides.title: ESUN scope and focus
slides.subtitle: Narrow on purpose. Ethernet framing + switching for scale-up. Nothing else.

bullets.text: L2/L3 framing, efficient headers, link-level error recovery, lossless transport.
host transport, application layer, non-Ethernet protocols.
clear split between networking and host functionality — vendors converge without dictating accelerator architecture.
baseline so vendors can build and ship now. Backward compatibility committed for future versions.
transport features evolving in UEC stay out of v1.0 until they stabilize.

figure.alt: ESUN technical focus areas: L2/L3 framing, efficient headers, error recovery, lossless transport
figure.caption: XPUs sit on top of transports (OCP SUE Transport in green). ESUN governs Ethernet Header, Data Link, and PHY. Source: OCP ESUN Summit deck.

slides.title: Replacing the IP header with EH

figure.alt: Silent animation showing a standard Ethernet frame's 20–40 byte IP header contracting into the 4-byte ESUN Header while Ethernet framing and payload remain.
figure.caption: Standard Ethernet/IP transitions to ESUN: 20–40 bytes of IP header are removed, a 4-byte ESUN Header is added, and compact fields restore the controls the scale-up domain needs.

slides.title: The ESUN Header, field by field

figure.alt: ESUN Header bit layout: Rev (2b), F, EH-CoS (3b), EH-ECN (2b), Flow label (16b), TTL (4b), UD (2b), RSVD (2b)
figure.caption: 32 bits total. Rev (2b) = 00 for v1.0. F gates the Flow Label. EH-CoS replaces 802.1p/DSCP; EH-ECN encoded like IP-TOS ECN. Flow Label adds hashing entropy. TTL prevents loops. UD is end-to-end. Switches that mutate ECN or TTL must recompute FCS.

slides.title: ESUN vs standard Ethernet — the fabric story

figure.alt: Comparison: Standard Ethernet vs ESUN across Link Level CM, Fabric Feedback, Traffic Differentiation, Resilience and Load Balancing, Multi-Hop Topology
figure.caption: Transient congestion: PFC + CBFC. Persistent: EH-ECN. Differentiation: EH-CoS. Resilience: LLR + EH-Flow Label. Multi-hop: EH-TTL + EH-ECN. CBFC and LLR are borrowed from UEC.

source_refs.section: 5.3 Lossless, differentiated and resilient Hierarchical Topologies

slides.title: Endpoint and switch requirements
slides.subtitle: Plus: forwarding is static DA-based; security uses MACsec at L2 (IPSec no longer applies).
rows: From UEC; recovers from link BER locally
Takes precedence over 802.1Q CoS and CBFC TC
Drop at 0; FCS recompute on change

slides.title: Where to go next
slides.subtitle: Three takeaways: ESUN evolves Ethernet, not replaces it · the 4-byte EH is the central move · PFC+CBFC, EH-ECN, EH-CoS, LLR, Flow Label, and TTL form a complete fabric story. Version 1.1 is already in development, with backward compatibility committed.

slides.title: Check Your ESUN Mental Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the central packet-format change ESUN makes for scale-up traffic?

choices.text: It replaces the IP header with a 4-byte ESUN Header while keeping Ethernet framing.
It adds a larger IP extension header so switches can keep using IP routing fields.
choices.feedback_incorrect: ESUN moves in the other direction: it removes the IP header for this scale-up domain and restores only the needed fields in a smaller ESUN Header.

choices.text: It replaces Ethernet with a proprietary accelerator-only fabric protocol.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The course framing is that ESUN evolves Ethernet rather than replacing it, preserving Ethernet optics, silicon, and operational tooling.

choices.text: It requires VLAN tagging for every scale-up packet to carry class of service.
choices.feedback_incorrect: ESUN is designed for scale-up domains that do not need VLANs; EH-CoS carries class-of-service information in the ESUN Header.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. ESUN keeps Ethernet framing but removes 20-40 bytes of IP header overhead and adds a compact 4-byte ESUN Header.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the header-efficiency slide. ESUN keeps Ethernet framing and replaces the IP header with a compact ESUN Header.

questions.prompt: Which behaviors are required for ESUN switches? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Support PFC, CBFC, and LLR with per-port configurability where specified.
Use the Flow Label for hashing when the F bit is set.
Decrement EH-TTL during forwarding and drop packets whose TTL reaches zero.
Broadcast ESUN packets with unknown destination addresses until MAC learning converges.
Apply IPSec directly to the ESUN Header for link confidentiality.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. ESUN switches carry the lossless/resilience features, hash on Flow Label when valid, and use EH-TTL to prevent loops.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Focus on the conformance table: ESUN switches use static destination-address forwarding, must not broadcast unknown ESUN destinations, and use MACsec rather than IPSec for Layer 2 link security.

term_glossary.tooltip: Ethernet for Scale-Up Networks; OCP's Ethernet evolution for scale-up AI infrastructure.
Low-latency fabric that lets GPUs or XPUs act like one tightly coupled accelerator domain.
Small, frequent synchronization and data-exchange traffic used when a model is split across accelerators.
Useful payload delivered after protocol overhead is removed.
Ultra Ethernet Consortium; source of CBFC and LLR mechanisms referenced by ESUN.
The 4-byte header that replaces the IP header in ESUN packets.
New EtherType used to identify ESUN Headers; value is pending assignment.
3-bit ESUN class-of-service field replacing DSCP and 802.1p-style traffic differentiation.
2-bit congestion notification field encoded like IP ECN.
16-bit source-set entropy field for deterministic load balancing.
4-bit Time To Live field decremented each hop to prevent loops.
Priority Flow Control for transient link-level congestion.
Credit-Based Flow Control; UEC-defined receiver-credit mechanism.
Link Level Retry; UEC-defined local recovery for link-level errors.
Layer 2 point-to-point encryption path recommended because ESUN removes the IP header.
IP-layer security that no longer applies directly once ESUN replaces the IP header.
